check: Every migration has a rollback path
description: Write and test the down/rollback migration alongside the up migration, even if you don't expect to use it
why_it_matters: A bad deploy without a rollback path turns a 5-minute fix into an incident — the rollback is insurance you write before you need it, not after

check: No irreversible destructive drops in a single step
description: Drop a column/table only after a deprecation window (stop writing to it, confirm nothing reads it, then drop in a later migration) rather than in the same release that stops using it
why_it_matters: A column dropped in the same deploy that removes its last usage can't be restored if that release turns out to have a bug depending on it

check: NOT NULL columns added with a default or backfill
description: Adding a NOT NULL column to a table with existing rows requires either a DEFAULT value or a backfill step before the constraint is enforced
why_it_matters: Without one of these, the migration fails outright against existing data, or worse, silently breaks existing insert code paths that don't set the new column

check: Column rename done as add+backfill+drop, not in-place
description: Add the new column, backfill it, update application code to write both, then drop the old column in a later deploy — never rename in place on a live table
why_it_matters: An in-place rename breaks any in-flight application code (old pods/instances) still referencing the old column name until they've all redeployed

check: Large-table backfills run in batches
description: Backfilling a new column or migrating data on a large table is done in small batched transactions (e.g. 1,000 rows at a time), not one giant transaction
why_it_matters: A single massive transaction locks the table for its full duration, and a failure partway through means starting over instead of resuming from the last completed batch

check: New indexes created CONCURRENTLY (Postgres)
description: Use `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` for indexes added to tables receiving live writes
why_it_matters: A normal `CREATE INDEX` takes a lock that blocks writes to the table for the duration of the index build, which can be minutes on a large table

check: Foreign keys added as NOT VALID then VALIDATE separately
description: On a large existing table, add the foreign key constraint with NOT VALID first (fast, no full-table lock), then run VALIDATE CONSTRAINT as a separate step
why_it_matters: Adding a foreign key normally scans and locks the whole table to verify existing rows; splitting it into two steps avoids the long lock on the initial add

check: In-flight application code tolerates the new schema during rollout
description: Plan every migration so that both the old and new versions of the application code can run correctly against the schema mid-deploy (rolling deploys mean both versions run simultaneously for a period)
why_it_matters: A migration that only the *new* code can handle correctly will break requests served by old-code instances still running during a rolling deploy

check: Migrations run automatically via CI/CD, not manually
description: Migrations apply as an automated step in the deploy pipeline (see devops module, Phase 3) rather than a developer running them by hand against production
why_it_matters: Manual migration application is error-prone (wrong environment, forgotten step, out-of-order application) and isn't auditable the way a pipeline run is

check: Migration files are never edited after being applied anywhere shared
description: Once a migration has run against staging or production, treat the file as immutable — make a new migration for any further change, never edit history
why_it_matters: Editing an already-applied migration desyncs environments that already ran the old version from ones that will run the edited version, corrupting the migration history's guarantee

check: Schema migrations kept separate from data backfill scripts
description: Structural changes (add column, add index) live in versioned migration files; one-off data fixes/backfills are separate scripts, not mixed into the same file
why_it_matters: Mixing them makes migrations slower to run repeatedly (e.g. in tests, which re-run all migrations) and harder to reason about what a migration actually changes structurally

check: Staging mirrors production schema before applying to prod
description: Every migration runs successfully against a staging environment with production-representative data volume before it's applied to production
why_it_matters: Catches lock-duration issues, constraint violations against real data, and performance problems that a nearly-empty local dev database would never surface

check: Long-running migrations scheduled during low-traffic windows
description: A migration expected to take more than a few seconds (even with CONCURRENTLY/NOT VALID techniques) is scheduled and communicated, not run silently during peak traffic
why_it_matters: Even a well-designed migration can have unexpected lock contention under real production load — minimize blast radius by timing it deliberately

check: Backups verified before any destructive migration
description: Confirm a recent, restorable backup exists immediately before running any migration that drops, truncates, or irreversibly transforms data
why_it_matters: A migration bug discovered after the fact is only recoverable if a good backup exists — verify this before, not after, something goes wrong

check: Native enum value additions checked for compatibility
description: Postgres native ENUM types can have values added but not easily removed or reordered in a single transaction in older Postgres versions — confirm your Postgres version's behavior before relying on this
why_it_matters: Assuming enum values can be freely added/removed like a lookup table's rows leads to migrations that fail or require workarounds mid-deploy

check: RLS policies updated in the same migration as the table they protect
description: When altering a table's structure in a way that affects what a policy checks, update the RLS policy in the same migration, not a follow-up one
why_it_matters: A structural change landing before its corresponding policy update leaves a window where the table's access control doesn't match its actual schema/intent

check: Migration rollback tested in a non-production environment
description: Before considering a rollback path 'safe,' actually run it against a staging environment with realistic data, don't just assume the down migration works because it was written
why_it_matters: An untested rollback migration is a false sense of safety — it may fail, or worse, silently corrupt data differently than expected

check: Composite/multi-column ALTER statements reviewed for lock behavior
description: Multiple ALTER TABLE operations combined in one statement can escalate lock requirements — review what lock level Postgres actually takes for the combined operation, not just each part individually
why_it_matters: Some combinations of otherwise-safe individual ALTERs require a stronger lock when combined into a single statement than either would alone

check: Migrations committed to supabase/migrations/, applied via CLI
description: Manage schema changes as versioned SQL files in `supabase/migrations/`, applied with `supabase db push` / the Supabase MCP `apply_migration` tool — never hand-edit schema directly in the dashboard for anything meant to be reproducible
why_it_matters: Dashboard-only changes aren't captured in version control, can't be code-reviewed, and won't be replayed when spinning up a new environment or branch

check: Seed data kept separate from schema migrations
description: Reference/seed data (e.g. default categories, plan tiers) lives in a `seed.sql` file, not embedded inside structural migration files
why_it_matters: Keeps migrations focused on structure, and lets seed data be re-run independently (e.g. to reset a dev/staging environment) without re-running structural changes

check: Migration reviewed by a second person before applying to production
description: Treat a migration PR with the same review rigor as application code — a second set of eyes on lock behavior, rollback correctness, and data-integrity impact
why_it_matters: Migrations are uniquely hard to fully undo once applied to a shared environment with real data — the cost of a missed issue is higher than in most code review
